title: Insurance Agencies and Brokerages

labor_share.method: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 524210 ($103,879,237,202) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 524210 ($207,109,590,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

occupations.title: Insurance Claims and Policy Processing Clerks
First-Line Supervisors of Office and Administrative Support Workers
Secretaries and Administrative Assistants, Except Legal, Medical, and Executive
Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists

n_band.derivation: margin 0.2357 (margins.json[524]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Insurance (General) EBITDA/Sales 23.57%); revenue/employee $256,440 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 524210); class <5: 91,066 firms, avg revenue/firm $323,793 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $76,318 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 20,081 firms, avg revenue/firm $929,879 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $219,173 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 5,672 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,313,385 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $545,265 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 2,689 firms, avg revenue/firm $8,126,954 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,915,523 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 547 firms, avg revenue/firm $40,097,678 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $9,451,023 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 379 firms, avg revenue/firm $269,239,583 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $63,459,770 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 3,236

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Quoting, application data entry, carrier comparison shopping and proposal assembly automate heavily (AI quoting/submission tools live from Applied/Vertafore/insurtech vendors). But wage bill skews to commercial producers whose relationship selling, complex risk placement and renewal retention conversations persist; personal-lines producers are far more exposed than middle-market commercial. Blended across lines of business.

Certificates of insurance, endorsements, policy checking, renewal remarketing and carrier correspondence are the industry's biggest AI target and are automating fast; client advocacy in claims and complex-service escalations stay human.

breakdown_by_role.role: Insurance Claims and Policy Processing Clerks (43-9041)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Pure document/data workflow — near-fully automatable with current AI plus carrier API/download connectivity; residual human share for exception handling and carrier-portal fragmentation.
Book analytics, carrier-relationship reporting and ops dashboards automate substantially; carrier contract negotiation, producer recruitment and P&L ownership persist.

Desk adjudication of routine claims automates substantially (document intake, coverage matching, estimate review); field inspection and negotiation persist. Mostly present in MGA/TPA-flavored firms within the code.
Generic document handling, filing and data entry — near-fully automatable.
MGA/MGU delegated-underwriting sub-segment: risk triage, file assembly and data pre-fill automate; appetite judgment and carrier accountability persist.

breakdown_by_role.role: First-Line Supervisors of Office and Administrative Support Workers (43-1011)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Workflow monitoring and QA automate partially; people management persists (and shrinks only as the supervised pool shrinks).
Scheduling, correspondence and document prep — heavily automatable with current AI assistants.

breakdown_by_role.role: Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists (13-1161)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Lead generation, cross-sell campaign content and analytics automate near-fully; brand/relationship marketing partially persists.

breakdown_by_role.role: Unlisted tail (account managers, accounting/commission reconciliation, compliance, IT, HR, misc admin)
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Per OEWS residual: mix of account-manager roles (~8-12pp, partial ~0.55-0.6 like CSRs), accounting/commission reconciliation and back-office admin (heavily automatable ~0.6-0.7), and lower-automatable professional/IT/HR slivers. Blended at 0.50 to avoid winner-anchor inflation.

pi_dist.rationale: The agency legally owns expirations/renewals — the industry's core asset doctrine — and acquired books renew at high rates, so the firm (not a platform or procurement desk) controls the client. Revenue is a carrier-set commission on premium (client pays the carrier, not the agency), so AI cost savings cannot be competed away through client fee pressure in the core model: efficiency drops to the operator's margin dollar-for-dollar. Two leaks keep this below ~0.9: (1) carriers can trim commission schedules over time as their own AI reduces what they need agents for; (2) fee-based large-commercial accounts do see and negotiate the cost; (3) producer book portability — books partially walk with producers despite non-piracy agreements.

pi_moat.rationale: Every producer must hold state licenses (NIPR); AI cannot legally bind coverage or advise in most states without a licensed human — real but shallow, since carriers hold licenses too and can go direct. Switching costs are moderate (renewal inertia, carrier appointments, historical loss data). Against this: personal lines already proved direct distribution wins when the interaction is simple (GEICO/Progressive), embedded and AI-native distribution exists, and carriers could underwrite small-commercial direct as AI simplifies intake. Independent-agency share has held in commercial lines because of carrier appetite fragmentation and risk complexity, not statute.

t50_years.derivation: current_adoption_pct ~30% material use (22% 'limited areas' + 8% 'embedded in daily workflows' per 2026 Big 'I' ACT Tech Trends survey), with two-thirds of agencies planning to increase AI use within 12 months. Historical agency-tech analogs (AMS to near-universal, personal-lines comparative raters to ~57%) took ~10-15 years to majority, but GenAI adoption in this document-heavy vertical has gone 0->30% in roughly 3 years (2023-2026), a much steeper curve, and the vendor channel (Applied/Vertafore AMS duopoly shipping embedded AI) removes the integration barrier that slowed prior tech. Extrapolating the current slope (~7-8pp/yr, accelerating per stated intent): 20pp remaining to 50% implies ~2-3 years. t50 = 2.5.

current_adoption_pct.source: Big 'I' Agents Council for Technology (ACT) 2026 Tech Trends Report, via Agency Checklists / Insurance Journal
current_adoption_pct.figure_quoted: 31% of agencies reported they are not currently using AI, 33% 'just experimenting', 22% using AI in limited areas, 8% embedded in daily workflows; two-thirds plan to increase AI use in next 12 months. Material use = limited-area use or deeper in core workflows: 22% + 8% = 30%.

historical_analogs.value: Agency management systems reached >90% penetration but over multiple decades; personal-lines comparative raters reached ~57% of agencies roughly 15 years after mainstream introduction (Catalyit 2024 research); real-time/download carrier connectivity took 10+ years to majority. Comparable prior technology: ~10-15 years to ~50%.
historical_analogs.source: Catalyit 2024 research via First Connect / Nationwide Agency Forward; Big 'I' Agency Universe Study AMS penetration
historical_analogs.figure_quoted: 'personal-lines rating platforms are used by 57 percent of agencies' (Catalyit 2024); 'more than 9 out of 10 independent agencies in the U.S. reporting use of an AMS'

owners_60plus_pct.source: 2023 Agency Growth Study (Liberty Mutual / Safeco / State Auto), reported by IA Magazine
owners_60plus_pct.figure_quoted: '40% of the agency principals surveyed are older than 60 years old and 20% are older than 65 years old. Half of agencies will experience a major change of ownership in the next 10 years.' (survey of 450+ agency principals/owners)

succession_shortage_documented.evidence: Aging ownership is well documented (40% of principals over 60), but a successor shortage is not: the Big 'I' Agency Universe Study reports more than 8 in 10 agencies have a perpetuation plan, and the deep external buyer pool means exits clear readily. No source reviewed documents a shortage of successors/buyers.

active_consolidators.source: OPTIS Partners year-end 2025 M&A report, via Insurance Journal / AccessNewswire
active_consolidators.figure_quoted: 695 agency deals in 2025 (down 12% from 2024); private-equity-backed/hybrid brokers accounted for 72% of all transactions; top buyers BroadStreet Partners, Hub International (49 deals), Inszone (45), World, Keystone Agency Partners, Leavitt. OPTIS's buyer tables show roughly 25-35 PE-backed platforms completing multiple add-ons across 2024-2025; 30 used as the count of active PE-backed platforms in the last 24 months.

buy_mult.source: CT Acquisitions Insurance Agency M&A Multiples Report 2026 (aggregating MarshBerry quarterly, OPTIS Partners, GF Data)
buy_mult.figure_quoted: '7.0x to 9.0x adjusted EBITDA' for lower-middle-market brokerages ($2-10M revenue); '9.0x to 12.0x adjusted EBITDA' for regional platforms ($10-50M revenue). The $1-10M EBITDA band spans both tiers (~$4-40M revenue at ~24% margin); 9.5x taken as the band median. Adjusted-EBITDA basis (post market-rate owner comp, contingents normalized).

exit_mult.source: MarshBerry valuation commentary; CT Acquisitions 2026 multiples report
exit_mult.figure_quoted: 'platform firms have held flat at an average of around 14x EBITDA for base purchase price at closing, and 19x with an earn-out' (MarshBerry); PE-backed platform primary transactions '12.0x to 16.0x adjusted EBITDA' (CT Acquisitions). 14.0x base used, excluding earn-out upside for conservatism.

pricing_structure.value: ~90%+ of core retail-agency revenue is carrier-set commission on premium: 10-15% of premium on new P&C business, 8-12% on renewals, varying 5-20% by line; contingent/profit-sharing commissions add roughly 2-5% of premium equivalent (volatile, buyer-haircut). Fee-based compensation (in lieu of or on top of commission) is confined mostly to large-commercial/benefits accounts — a small minority of revenue for the $1-10M EBITDA retail agency. Clients pay the carrier, not the agency, so agency operating costs are invisible to the client.
pricing_structure.source: Agentero commission-structure guide; SIAA of Arizona commission-rate explainer
pricing_structure.figure_quoted: 'average agency-level commission is 10% to 15% of premium on new business and 8% to 12% on renewals'; 'Contingent commissions can add 2-5% of total premium to an agency's revenue'

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: V_raw 0.2120 implies up to ~21.2pp of revenue freed as EBITDA uplift at full automation of the declared tasks — far above the 3pp floor, so no tension with A = 4.0 (t50 = 2.5y). Realized uplift will phase in with adoption, but the ceiling is large and consistent with a fast adoption incentive.

terminal_value.justification: AI cutting agency costs (service/processing labor) is real and large, and distinct from AI replacing the product (matching insureds to carriers). Demand is held by agency ownership of renewals, licensed-intermediary requirements, and carrier appetite fragmentation — none of which is a statute protecting the channel. Personal lines already proved direct wins when interaction is simple (GEICO/Progressive), and AI plausibly makes small-commercial simple enough for carriers/AI-native platforms to go direct over 10+ years, eroding that commission pool. But the $1-10M EBITDA band firms are predominantly commercial/benefits agencies whose books skew to complexity-protected middle-market commercial risk (fragmented carrier appetite, negotiated placement, claims advocacy) — a majority of the target book by revenue. Commission pools in personal/small-commercial shrink; middle-market commercial placement demand persists. Net: shrinking but surviving demand — PRESSURED, not MELTING.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: Confirmed. V was derived solely from the provided labor_share and role_mix plus within-role automatable fractions grounded in task content (quoting, COIs, endorsements, policy checking, claims desk work). A was derived solely from the Big 'I' ACT adoption survey and agency-tech adoption analogs (AMS, comparative raters). No deal counts, consolidator activity, or M&A trends were used for V or A; OPTIS/MarshBerry data informed only B (crowding via CFD) and M (multiples).
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Billing is overwhelmingly carrier-set commission on premium (10-15% new / 8-12% renewal) plus volatile contingents; fee-for-service is a small minority confined to large-commercial/benefits accounts. Because the client pays the carrier and never sees the agency's cost base, AI efficiency accrues to the operator as margin rather than deflating to clients — the opposite of cost-plus/hourly deflation. This supports the high pi_dist = 0.85; the residual leaks (carriers trimming commission schedules over time, fee-negotiated large accounts, producer book portability) are why pi_dist is not 0.9+, and channel-bypass risk (carriers/insurtechs going direct as AI simplifies placement) is priced in pi_moat = 0.60.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A ranges 2.5 (t50=4) to 6.67 (t50=1.5); S ranges ~4.28 to ~5.22 holding all else constant. Largest single swing factor in S.

M ranges 3.0 (buy=8.0) down to 0.87 (buy=11.5, near-converged spread); S ranges ~5.17 down to ~4.03. The decade-long buyer frenzy may have compressed the band-firm entry price more than the 9.5x estimate reflects.
ai_replaceable_share ranges ~0.33-0.47, V_raw 0.174-0.250, V 6.98-9.99; S ranges ~4.52 to ~4.87.
C ranges 4.25-5.95; S ranges ~4.54 to ~4.86, driven by how fast carriers/AI-native platforms can take small-commercial direct.

reviewer_note: Heterogeneous code scored on the dominant sub-segment: retail independent P&C/benefits agencies (the Big 'I'/Reagan survey universe). 524210 also contains MGAs/MGUs, wholesale brokers, life/annuity shops and personal-lines-only agencies with different economics and AI exposure (MGAs carry underwriters; personal-lines-only books are far more terminal-exposed) — flagged heterogeneous:true for that reason even though the code is not a formal catch-all. Key tensions: (1) M is the binding constraint — this is the most consolidator-crowded services vertical in the US and entry multiples for $1-10M EBITDA firms (9-12x per MarshBerry-derived tiers) have converged well toward the ~14x platform exit, leaving M=1.89; the classic roll-up arbitrage is mostly gone here and S=4.71 reflects that despite a near-max V. (2) The n_band derivation uses Damodaran 'Insurance (General)' margin 23.57%, which is a carrier-flavored composite; agency-specific EBITDA margins (Reagan OGP ~20-25% for well-run agencies) are similar, so the band count is not materially distorted — used as given per rule 6. (3) succession_shortage_documented set to false deliberately: ownership is old (40% of principals 60+) but perpetuation plans are widespread (>80% per Agency Universe Study) and the buyer pool is deep, so no shortage is documented — OW stays at 0.8571 without the +0.1 bonus. (4) P&C rate cycle: 2023-2025 hard-market premium growth inflated commission revenue and TTM EBITDA; a softening cycle would shave margins — buyers already haircut contingents, reflected in using base (not earn-out) multiples. (5) current_adoption_pct counts limited-area use as material; if only 'embedded in daily workflows' (8%) counted, t50 stretches toward 4y and A falls to 2.5 — captured in the t50 range.
